---
title: "MULTIPLE LINEAR REGRESSION ANALYSIS OF JET TURBINE ENGINE THRUST"
output:
  word_document: default
  html_document: default
  pdf_document: default
date: "2025-12-23"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## 1. Introduction🚀

This report presents a statistical analysis of the *turbine thrust* dataset (`turbine_thrust.XLS`). The dataset contains one response variable `y` and six predictor variables `x1` to `x6`, representing engineering or operational measurements related to turbine performance. The objective is to explore relationships, fit a regression model, and interpret results using **R**.

![](turbprp.gif){width="231"}

## 2. Data Preparation and Loading⚙️

The data-set was imported into R using the `readxl` package. Missing values, outliers, and data structure were checked.

```{r cars}


# Clear workspace
rm(list = ls())


data <- data.frame(
  THRUST = c(4540, 4315, 4095, 3650, 3200, 4833, 4617, 4340, 3820, 3368,
             4445, 4188, 3981, 3622, 3125, 4560, 4340, 4115, 3630, 3210,
             4330, 4119, 3891, 3467, 3045, 4411, 4203, 3968, 3531, 3074,
             4350, 4128, 3940, 3480, 3064, 4402, 4180, 3973, 3530, 3080),
  PSR = c(2140, 2016, 1905, 1675, 1474, 2239, 2120, 1990, 1702, 1487,
          2107, 1973, 1864, 1674, 1440, 2165, 2048, 1916, 1658, 1489,
          2062, 1929, 1815, 1595, 1400, 2047, 1935, 1807, 1591, 1388,
          2071, 1944, 1831, 1612, 1410, 2066, 1954, 1835, 1616, 1407),
  SSR = c(20640, 20280, 19860, 18980, 18100, 20740, 20305, 19961, 18916, 18012,
          20520, 20130, 19780, 19020, 18030, 20680, 20340, 19860, 18950, 18700,
          20500, 20050, 19680, 18890, 17870, 20540, 20160, 19750, 18890, 17870,
          20460, 20010, 19640, 18710, 17780, 20520, 20150, 19750, 18850, 17910),
  FFR = c(30250, 30010, 29780, 29330, 28960, 30083, 29831, 29604, 29088, 28675,
          30120, 29920, 29720, 29370, 28940, 30160, 29960, 29710, 29250, 28890,
          30190, 29960, 29770, 29360, 28960, 30160, 29940, 29760, 29350, 28910,
          30180, 29940, 29750, 29360, 28900, 30170, 29950, 29740, 29320, 28910),
  P = c(205, 195, 184, 164, 144, 216, 206, 196, 171, 149,
        195, 190, 180, 161, 139, 208, 199, 187, 164, 145,
        193, 183, 173, 153, 134, 193, 184, 173, 153, 133,
        198, 186, 178, 156, 136, 197, 188, 178, 156, 137),
  ET = c(1732, 1697, 1662, 1598, 1541, 1709, 1669, 1640, 1572, 1522,
         1740, 1711, 1682, 1630, 1572, 1704, 1679, 1642, 1576, 1528,
         1748, 1713, 1684, 1624, 1569, 1746, 1714, 1679, 1621, 1561,
         1729, 1692, 1667, 1609, 1552, 1758, 1729, 1690, 1616, 1569),
  AT = c(99, 100, 97, 97, 97, 87, 87, 87, 85, 85,
         101, 100, 100, 100, 101, 98, 96, 94, 94, 94,
         101, 100, 100, 99, 100, 99, 99, 99, 99, 99,
         102, 101, 101, 101, 101, 100, 99, 99, 99, 100)
)



print(dim(data))
print(head(data))
print(summary(data))





```

A)  **Fit a multiple regression model to the data and test the significance of the individual coefficients as well as the overall regression model.**🔬

```{r}
# Fit full model
model <- lm(THRUST ~ PSR + SSR + FFR + P + ET + AT, data = data)

print(summary(model))


# Individual coefficient tests
cat("\n3. INDIVIDUAL COEFFICIENT TESTS:\n")
coef_table <- summary(model)$coefficients
for(i in 1:nrow(coef_table)) {
  cat(sprintf("%-10s: t = %7.3f, p-value = %.4f %s\n",
              rownames(coef_table)[i],
              coef_table[i, 3],
              coef_table[i, 4],
              ifelse(coef_table[i, 4] < 0.001, "***",
                     ifelse(coef_table[i, 4] < 0.01, "**",
                            ifelse(coef_table[i, 4] < 0.05, "*",
                                   ifelse(coef_table[i, 4] < 0.1, ".", ""))))))
}
```

## **Interpretation:**🧠

**Excellent Model Fit:** - **R² = 0.9977**: Model explains **99.77%** of thrust variation (near-perfect fit) - **Adjusted R² = 0.9972**: Minimal penalty for 6 predictors (no overfitting) - **F-statistic = 2350, p \< 0.0001**: Overall model is highly significant

#### **2. SIGNIFICANT PREDICTORS:**

**Highly Significant (α = 0.05):** 1. **PSR (Primary Speed):** β = 1.119, p = 0.0004 - **Strong positive effect**: Main controllable factor for thrust

2.  **AT (Ambient Temperature):** β = -16.95, p \< 0.0001
    -   **Strong negative effect**: Most statistically significant
    -   Each degree increase reduces thrust by \~17 units
3.  **ET (Exhaust Temperature):** β = 0.822, p = 0.0253
    -   **Positive effect**: Counterintuitive - needs investigation
    -   May indicate measurement issues or complex relationships

**Not Significant:** - **SSR (Secondary Speed):** p = 0.4355 - **P (Pressure):** p = 0.1612 - **FFR (Fuel Flow Rate):** p = 0.0592 (marginally significant)

# b)COMPUTE AND INTERPRET R²

```{r}
r_squared <- summary(model)$r.squared
adj_r_squared <- summary(model)$adj.r.squared

print(sprintf("Multiple R-squared    : %.4f (%.1f%% of variation explained)\n",
            r_squared, r_squared*100))
print(sprintf("Adjusted R-squared   : %.4f\n", adj_r_squared))

# Calculate manually to verify
SSR <- sum((fitted(model) - mean(data$THRUST))^2)
SST <- sum((data$THRUST - mean(data$THRUST))^2)
SSE <- sum(residuals(model)^2)
r2_manual <- 1 - SSE/SST

print(sprintf("Manual calculation   : R² = 1 - SSE/SST = 1 - %.1f/%.1f = %.4f\n",
            SSE, SST, r2_manual))
```

Interpretation: **Model explains 99.8% of the data's variation** — an almost perfect fit.

-   **R² = 0.9977**: Our predictors are extremely strong at explaining the outcome
-   **Adjusted R² = 0.9972**: Virtually the same, meaning we are not overfitting with unnecessary variables
-   **Manual check confirms**: Calculation is correct ✅

**Note**: Such near-perfect results are rare outside fields like physics or engineering. Double-check for:

 1. Data errors or circular calculations

2. Whether this makes sense for my problem

3\. Testing on new data to confirm it's not too good to be true

# c)NORMAL PROBABILITY PLOT OF RESIDUALS

```{r}

# Set up plotting area
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

# Normal probability plot
residuals <- residuals(model)
n <- length(residuals)
ordered_residuals <- sort(residuals)
expected_normal <- qnorm((1:n - 0.5)/n)

plot(expected_normal, ordered_residuals,
     xlab = "Theoretical Quantiles",
     ylab = "Sample Residuals",
     main = "Normal Q-Q Plot of Residuals",
     pch = 19, col = "blue")
abline(a = 0, b = sd(residuals), col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Shapiro-Wilk test for normality
shapiro_test <- shapiro.test(residuals)
cat(sprintf("\nShapiro-Wilk normality test:\n"))
cat(sprintf("W = %.4f, p-value = %.4f\n", 
            shapiro_test$statistic, shapiro_test$p.value))

if(shapiro_test$p.value > 0.05) {
  cat("Conclusion: No evidence to reject normality assumption (p > 0.05)\n")
} else {
  cat("Conclusion: Evidence suggests non-normal residuals (p ≤ 0.05)\n")
}

# Histogram of residuals
hist(residuals, breaks = 10,
     main = "Histogram of Residuals",
     xlab = "Residuals",
     col = "lightblue",
     probability = TRUE)
curve(dnorm(x, mean = mean(residuals), sd = sd(residuals)),
      add = TRUE, col = "red", lwd = 2)

```

## Interpretation: 🧠

✅ My data appears normal – I have no evidence against the normality assumption.

What this means for me: I can safely use statistical methods that assume normally distributed data (like t-tests, linear regression, ANOVA) for my analysis.

d)  **RESIDUALS VS PREDICTED PLOT**

```{r}

par(mfrow = c(1, 1), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

fitted_values <- fitted(model)
plot(fitted_values, residuals,
     xlab = "Predicted THRUST",
     ylab = "Residuals",
     main = "Residuals vs Predicted Values",
     pch = 19, col = "blue")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Add LOWESS smoother (base R version)
lowess_fit <- lowess(fitted_values, residuals)
lines(lowess_fit, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2)

# Test for heteroscedasticity (Breusch-Pagan test, manual calculation)
cat("\nChecking constant variance assumption:\n")
n <- nrow(data)
p <- length(coef(model)) - 1
residuals_sq <- residuals^2
aux_model <- lm(residuals_sq ~ PSR + SSR + FFR + P + ET + AT, data = data)
LM_stat <- n * summary(aux_model)$r.squared
p_value_BP <- 1 - pchisq(LM_stat, df = p)

cat(sprintf("Breusch-Pagan test (manual): LM = %.3f, p-value = %.4f\n",
            LM_stat, p_value_BP))

if(p_value_BP > 0.05) {
  cat("Conclusion: No evidence of heteroscedasticity (constant variance assumption reasonable)\n")
} else {
  cat("Conclusion: Evidence of heteroscedasticity (non-constant variance)\n")
}

```

## **Interpretation**:🧠

#####  ❌ **My data shows non-constant variance** – there is **clear evidence of heteroscedasticity** (p \< 0.05).

Looking at my residual plot, I can see clear signs of **heteroscedasticity** — the variance of the residuals changes with my predicted THRUST values.

**Key observations:** - **At lower predicted THRUST (\~3000)**: My residuals are tightly clustered (small spread) - **At higher predicted THRUST (\~4500)**: My residuals are much more spread out (larger variance)

**What this means:**\
As my predicted thrust values increase, the **prediction error also increases**. This "fanning out" pattern visually confirms the Breusch-Pagan test result and violates the constant variance assumption for linear regression.

# **e)SCALED RESIDUALS:** 

• Studentized standardized residuals

• Studentized residuals

• R-student residuals

```{r}


# Calculate various residuals
X <- model.matrix(model)
H <- X %*% solve(t(X) %*% X) %*% t(X)  # Hat matrix
h <- diag(H)  # Leverage values
MSE <- sum(residuals^2) / (n - p - 1)  # Mean squared error

# 1. Studentized standardized residuals (internally studentized)
r_i <- residuals / sqrt(MSE * (1 - h))

# 2. R-student residuals (externally studentized)
# Remove each observation and recompute
r_student <- numeric(n)
for(i in 1:n) {
  data_minus_i <- data[-i, ]
  model_minus_i <- lm(THRUST ~ PSR + SSR + FFR + P + ET + AT, data = data_minus_i)
  pred_i <- predict(model_minus_i, newdata = data[i, ])
  residual_i <- data$THRUST[i] - pred_i
  X_minus_i <- model.matrix(model_minus_i)
  H_minus_i <- X_minus_i %*% solve(t(X_minus_i) %*% X_minus_i) %*% t(X_minus_i)
  h_ii_minus <- diag(H_minus_i)[1]  # For the single prediction
  MSE_minus_i <- sum(residuals(model_minus_i)^2) / (n - p - 2)
  r_student[i] <- residual_i / sqrt(MSE_minus_i * (1 + h_ii_minus))
}

# Create diagnostic table
diagnostic_df <- data.frame(
  Observation = 1:n,
  Residual = round(residuals, 2),
  Standardized = round(scale(residuals), 3),
  Studentized = round(r_i, 3),
  R_Student = round(r_student, 3),
  Leverage = round(h, 4)
)

print("\nFirst 10 observations of diagnostic residuals:\n")
print(head(diagnostic_df, 10))

# Identify potential outliers
outliers_student <- which(abs(r_i) > 2)
outliers_rstudent <- which(abs(r_student) > 2)

print(sprintf("\nObservations with |Studentized residual| > 2: %s\n",
            paste(outliers_student, collapse = ", ")))
print(sprintf("Observations with |R-student| > 2: %s\n",
            paste(outliers_rstudent, collapse = ", ")))


```

## INTERPRETATION: 🧠

1\. Studentized standardized residuals: Account for unequal variance

2\. R-student residuals: More sensitive outlier detection (excludes observation)

3. Values \> \|2\| suggest potential outliers requiring investigation

f)PRESS STATISTIC AND PREDICTION R²

```{r}


# Calculate PRESS manually
press_residuals <- residuals / (1 - h)
PRESS <- sum(press_residuals^2)

# Calculate prediction R²
SST <- sum((data$THRUST - mean(data$THRUST))^2)
R2_pred <- 1 - (PRESS / SST)

print(sprintf("PRESS statistic     : %.2f\n", PRESS))
print(sprintf("Total SST          : %.2f\n", SST))
print(sprintf("Prediction R²      : %.4f\n", R2_pred))
print(sprintf("Model R²           : %.4f\n", r_squared))
print(sprintf("Difference         : %.4f\n", r_squared - R2_pred))

cat("\nINTERPRETATION:\n")
if(R2_pred > 0.9) {
  cat("Excellent predictive capability (R²_pred > 0.9)\n")
} else if(R2_pred > 0.7) {
  cat("Good predictive capability (R²_pred > 0.7)\n")
} else {
  cat("Moderate predictive capability\n")
}


```

### Excellent interpretation of my PRESS statistic results.🌟

**Key takeaway:** My model has **strong predictive power** on new data.

**What this tells me:**

-   **Prediction R² = 0.9948**: When predicting entirely new observations, my model still explains **99.5%** of the variation. This is excellent (\>0.9 is strong).

-   **Difference = 0.0029**: The tiny gap (0.29%) between my model's **fitting ability** (0.9977) and **prediction ability** (0.9948) means:

    -   My model is **not overfitting** significantly
    -   It should **generalize well** to new data

**Bottom line:** Despite the heteroscedasticity issue I identified earlier, my model demonstrates **excellent predictive capability** — it's not just fitting my current data well, but should perform reliably on future data too.

# **g)INFLUENCE DIAGNOSTICS:** 

• Hat diagonals

• Cook’s Distance (Cook’s D)

• DFBETAS • DFITS

```{r}


# 1. Hat diagonals (leverage)
leverage_cutoff1 <- 2 * (p + 1) / n
leverage_cutoff2 <- 3 * (p + 1) / n

# 2. Cook's Distance
cooks_d <- (r_i^2 / (p + 1)) * (h / (1 - h))
cooks_cutoff <- 4 / (n - p - 1)

# 3. DFBETAS
dfbetas_mat <- matrix(0, nrow = n, ncol = p + 1)
for(i in 1:n) {
  data_minus_i <- data[-i, ]
  model_minus_i <- lm(THRUST ~ PSR + SSR + FFR + P + ET + AT, data = data_minus_i)
  se_full <- summary(model)$coefficients[, 2]
  se_minus <- summary(model_minus_i)$coefficients[, 2]
  dfbetas_mat[i, ] <- (coef(model) - coef(model_minus_i)) / se_minus
}
dfbetas_cutoff <- 2 / sqrt(n)

# 4. DFITS
dffits_val <- r_i * sqrt(h / (1 - h))
dffits_cutoff <- 2 * sqrt((p + 1) / n)

# Create influence diagnostics data frame
influence_df <- data.frame(
  Observation = 1:n,
  Leverage = round(h, 4),
  CooksD = round(cooks_d, 4),
  DFITS = round(dffits_val, 4)
)

# Add DFBETAS for each predictor
dfbetas_names <- paste0("DFB_", names(coef(model)))
colnames(dfbetas_mat) <- dfbetas_names
influence_df <- cbind(influence_df, round(dfbetas_mat, 4))

print("\nINFLUENCE DIAGNOSTICS SUMMARY:\n")
print(sprintf("Sample size (n)     : %d\n", n))
print(sprintf("Parameters (p)      : %d\n", p))
print(sprintf("\nCutoff values:\n"))
cat(sprintf("  Leverage (2p/n)   : %.3f\n", leverage_cutoff1))
cat(sprintf("  Cook's D          : %.3f\n", cooks_cutoff))
cat(sprintf("  DFBETAS           : %.3f\n", dfbetas_cutoff))
cat(sprintf("  DFITS             : %.3f\n", dffits_cutoff))

# Identify influential observations
high_leverage <- which(h > leverage_cutoff1)
high_cooks <- which(cooks_d > cooks_cutoff)
high_dffits <- which(abs(dffits_val) > dffits_cutoff)

# Check DFBETAS for each predictor
high_dfbetas <- list()
for(j in 1:(p+1)) {
  high_dfbetas[[names(coef(model))[j]]] <- which(abs(dfbetas_mat[, j]) > dfbetas_cutoff)
}

cat("\nINFLUENTIAL OBSERVATIONS:\n")
cat(sprintf("High leverage (>%.3f)  : %s\n", leverage_cutoff1,
            paste(high_leverage, collapse = ", ")))
cat(sprintf("High Cook's D (>%.3f)  : %s\n", cooks_cutoff,
            paste(high_cooks, collapse = ", ")))
cat(sprintf("High DFITS (>%.3f)     : %s\n", dffits_cutoff,
            paste(high_dffits, collapse = ", ")))

cat("\nObservations influencing specific coefficients (DFBETAS >", dfbetas_cutoff, "):\n")
for(j in 1:(p+1)) {
  if(length(high_dfbetas[[j]]) > 0) {
    cat(sprintf("  %-10s: %s\n", names(coef(model))[j],
                paste(high_dfbetas[[j]], collapse = ", ")))
  }
}

# Create influence plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2, 1))

plot(h, pch = 19, col = "blue",
     main = "Leverage (Hat Diagonals)",
     ylab = "Leverage", xlab = "Observation")
abline(h = leverage_cutoff1, col = "red", lty = 2)
abline(h = leverage_cutoff2, col = "orange", lty = 2)
if(length(high_leverage) > 0) {
  text(high_leverage, h[high_leverage], labels = high_leverage, pos = 3, cex = 0.7)
}

plot(cooks_d, pch = 19, col = "blue",
     main = "Cook's Distance",
     ylab = "Cook's D", xlab = "Observation")
abline(h = cooks_cutoff, col = "red", lty = 2)
if(length(high_cooks) > 0) {
  text(high_cooks, cooks_d[high_cooks], labels = high_cooks, pos = 3, cex = 0.7)
}

plot(dffits_val, pch = 19, col = "blue",
     main = "DFITS",
     ylab = "DFITS", xlab = "Observation")
abline(h = c(-dffits_cutoff, dffits_cutoff), col = "red", lty = 2)
if(length(high_dffits) > 0) {
  text(high_dffits, dffits_val[high_dffits], labels = high_dffits, pos = 3, cex = 0.7)
}

# Plot DFBETAS for intercept and PSR (most significant)
plot(dfbetas_mat[, 1], pch = 19, col = "blue",
     main = "DFBETAS for Intercept",
     ylab = "DFBETAS", xlab = "Observation")
abline(h = c(-dfbetas_cutoff, dfbetas_cutoff), col = "red", lty = 2)



```

### **Influence diagnostics show several influential points in my model.**🔻

**Key findings:** - **Observations 11 and 20** consistently appear as highly influential across all diagnostics: - High leverage (\>0.350) - High Cook's D (\>0.121)\
- Influencing multiple coefficients

**What this means for my analysis:** - My model's results are **heavily influenced by a few key data points** (especially 11, 20, 21, 28) - If I remove or alter these observations, my regression coefficients could change substantially - This is concerning given I already identified heteroscedasticity issues

**Implications:** 1. My current model may be **too sensitive** to these specific observations 2. I should investigate **why these points are so influential** — are they data errors or legitimate extreme values? 3. Consider **robust regression methods** or verify these influential observations are valid

**Bottom line:** While my model has excellent R², its stability depends on a handful of observations that warrant careful examination.

# **FINAL SUMMARY AND CONCLUSIONS**

```{r}


# Model performance summary
cat("\nMODEL PERFORMANCE:\n")
cat(sprintf("1. R-squared               : %.4f (Excellent fit)\n", r_squared))
cat(sprintf("2. Adjusted R-squared      : %.4f\n", adj_r_squared))
cat(sprintf("3. Prediction R-squared    : %.4f (Good predictive ability)\n", R2_pred))
cat(sprintf("4. Residual Std. Error     : %.2f\n", summary(model)$sigma))

# Significance summary
cat("\nSIGNIFICANT PREDICTORS (α = 0.05):\n")
sig_predictors <- rownames(coef_table)[coef_table[, 4] < 0.05]
if(length(sig_predictors) > 0) {
  for(pred in sig_predictors) {
    if(pred != "(Intercept)") {
      direction <- ifelse(coef_table[pred, 1] > 0, "positive", "negative")
      cat(sprintf("  - %-10s: %s effect (p = %.4f)\n", 
                  pred, direction, coef_table[pred, 4]))
    }
  }
} else {
  cat("  None at α = 0.05 level\n")
}

# Assumptions check
cat("\nMODEL ASSUMPTIONS CHECK:\n")
cat("1. Normality: ")
if(shapiro_test$p.value > 0.05) {
  cat("Satisfied (Shapiro-Wilk p =", round(shapiro_test$p.value, 4), ")\n")
} else {
  cat("Questionable (consider transformations)\n")
}

cat("2. Constant variance: ")
if(p_value_BP > 0.05) {
  cat("Satisfied (Breusch-Pagan p =", round(p_value_BP, 4), ")\n")
} else {
  cat("Questionable (heteroscedasticity detected)\n")
}

cat("3. Influential observations: ")
if(length(high_cooks) == 0 && length(high_dffits) == 0) {
  cat("None detected\n")
} else {
  cat("Present (observations:", 
      paste(unique(c(high_cooks, high_dffits)), collapse = ", "), ")\n")
}

# Recommendations
cat("\nENGINEERING RECOMMENDATIONS:\n")
cat("1. Primary Speed (PSR) is the most significant positive predictor of thrust\n")
cat("2. Exhaust Temperature (ET) shows significant negative relationship\n")
cat("3. Focus on controlling PSR and monitoring ET for optimal performance\n")
cat("4. Consider model refinement by removing non-significant predictors\n")

cat("\nLIMITATIONS AND FURTHER ANALYSIS:\n")
cat("1. Consider interaction terms (e.g., PSR × ET)\n")
cat("2. Explore potential nonlinear relationships\n")
cat("3. Validate model with new data\n")
cat("4. Consider robust regression if outliers are problematic\n")



```
